target.title: Add JSDoc comments to CLI helper utilities.
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Five small CLI helper files had no JSDoc or inline comments, making the intent of non-obvious functions and regex patterns unclear to new contributors.
- Why it matters: Functions like `withManager`, `resolveOptionFromCommand`, `looksLikeLocalInstallSpec`, and `normalizeProfileName` have non-obvious behavior that benefits from a one-line explanation.
- What changed: Added JSDoc to functions in cli-utils.ts, command-format.ts, install-spec.ts, respawn-policy.ts, and profile-utils.ts. Added inline comments on three regex constants in command-format.ts and one in profile-utils.ts.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Local Docker builds support optional apt packages, but there is no equivalent setup/Dockerfile hook for installing Python packages needed by operator-managed scripts or skills. - Solution: Add an opt-in `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` build arg and pass it through `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. - What changed: Docker builds now install `python3-pip` only when pip packages are requested and `pip3` is missing, then install the requested pip packages with `pip3 install --no-cache-dir --break-system-packages`. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default Docker images and builds are unchanged when `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` is unset. Existing apt package build args are not changed. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Motivation Local Docker builds already support opt-in apt packages for operator-specific runtime needs. Some operator-managed scripts and skills also need Python packages that are not available as part of the default image. This adds a matching opt-in pip package hook while keeping default builds unchanged. - ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Local Docker builds can now install opt-in Python packages through `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES`. - Real environment tested: Local Docker build from this branch using `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: To run the docker setup with the new parameter: ```bash OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize" ./scripts/docker/setup.sh ``` To test that the requested packages have been installed: ```bash docker run --rm --entrypoint python3 openclaw:local -c 'import requests, humanize; print(requests.__version__); print(humanize.naturalsize(1234567)); print(requests.__file__); print(humanize.__file__)' ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): [1. target.title: fix(ui): keep delete confirm in viewport
target.body:
## Related issue

Related #50277

## Summary

- Position the chat delete confirmation popover relative to the viewport instead of the scroll container.
- Choose above or below the delete button based on available vertical space.
- Clamp the popover horizontally so it remains reachable on narrow viewports.
- Add focused jsdom geometry coverage for top-edge, bottom-edge, and narrow-width placement.

## User-visible behavior

The delete confirmation popover in Control UI chat should remain visible and reachable when deleting messages near the top or edges of the chat viewport.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI grouped chat delete confirmation could render out of reach when opened near the top or horizontal viewport edges because it was absolutely positioned inside the scrollable chat area. This patch positions the popover against the viewport, flips it above or below the delete button, and clamps it inside viewport margins.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `4a0465007c` on Raspberry Pi-class Linux host, Node `v22.21.1`, Chromium `142.0.7444.175`, using the PR's `.chat-delete-confirm` markup/CSS and viewport-placement code path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
chromium --headless=new --disable-gpu --no-sandbox --force-device-scale-factor=1 --window-size=360,640 --dump-dom file:///tmp/openclaw-delete-confirm-proof.html
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal output excerpt from Chromium:

```text
OpenClaw Control UI delete-confirm viewport proof
viewport=500x553
top-edge: placement=below left=90 top=38 withinViewport=true
bottom-edge: placement=above left=12 top=86 withinViewport=true
horizontal-edge: placement=below left=104 top=130 withinViewport=true
```

- Observed result after fix: In Chromium, the delete confirmation popover remains inside the viewport margins for top-edge, bottom-edge, and horizontal-edge cases. The top-edge case opens below the trigger, the bottom-edge case opens above the trigger, and the horizontal-edge case stays within the right boundary.
- What was not tested: Full gateway-backed dashboard click path on this Raspberry Pi-class host; dependency installation was intentionally avoided. Visual screenshot capture was not attached because copied browser output is the supplied proof.

## Supplemental validation

- `git diff --check` exited 0 locally with no whitespace errors.
- GitHub Actions `checks-node-core-ui` passed on head `4a0465007c`.
- GitHub Actions `check-test-types` passed on head `4a0465007c` after adding `this: HTMLElement` to the test geometry mock.

## AI-assisted

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the diff and understand the change scope.

target.title: fix(ui): prevent copy button showing CopyCopied! by using hidden attr
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #50756 — The copy button in code blocks displayed `CopyCopied!` instead of just `Copy`.

- **Problem:** The "Copied!" span inside the copy button was hidden purely via CSS (`display: none`). When CSS fails to load or loads late, both spans render simultaneously as "CopyCopied!".
- **Why it matters:** Users see broken UI text on code block copy buttons, especially on slower connections or certain browser caching behaviors.
- **What changed:** Added HTML `hidden` attribute to the `__done` span so it is invisible even before CSS loads. Updated the click handler to toggle `hidden` alongside the existing CSS class. Added `"hidden"` to the DOMPurify allowlist so the attribute survives sanitization.
- **What did NOT change:** CSS rules remain intact as a secondary styling layer; no markup structure changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #50756

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Code block copy button now correctly shows only "Copy" on initial render, regardless of CSS load timing.
- Click behavior unchanged: shows "Copied!" for 1.5s then reverts to "Copy".

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Added `"hidden"` to DOMPurify `allowedAttrs` — this is a safe, standard HTML boolean attribute with no XSS surface.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11
- Runtime: OpenClaw 3.13+
- Browser: MS Edge (Chromium)

### Steps

1. Start OpenClaw and open Control UI
2. Send a message that triggers a code block response
3. Observe the copy button text

### Expected

- Button shows "Copy"

### Actual (before fix)

- Button shows "CopyCopied!"

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- `cd ui && npx vitest run src/ui/markdown.test.ts` — all 16 tests pass including new regression test
- New test `copy button hides 'Copied!' text by default (#50756)` verifies the `hidden` attribute is present on `__done` and absent on `__idle`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Code block rendering with copy button in test environment
- Edge cases checked: DOMPurify sanitization preserves `hidden` attr; click toggle works correctly with both `hidden` and CSS class
- What I did **not** verify: Other browsers (Safari, Firefox) — but `hidden` is universally supported

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit; the CSS fallback still exists
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`, `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: Copy button text not toggling on click

## Risks and Mitigations

None — minimal change to a UI-only code path with regression test coverage.

target.title: fix: keep tui busy during follow-up waits
target.body:
## Summary

Keep the TUI visibly busy while a run is waiting on a follow-up event after `sessions_yield`.

Before this change, the TUI could appear idle even though work was still effectively in flight. The gap showed up when the final assistant message contained the `sessions_yield` follow-up context marker: the run had ended intentionally, but the UI status dropped back to an idle-looking state.

## What changed

- add `awaiting follow-up` as a busy TUI activity state
- detect the `sessions_yield` follow-up marker in final assistant output
- preserve `awaiting follow-up` when reloading session history
- clear stale `awaiting follow-up` state back to `idle` when history no longer contains the marker
- add focused tests for both live event handling and history restore behavior

## Files

- `src/tui/tui.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts`

## Validation

- direct smoke harness passed for:
  - final event -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - history restore -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - stale follow-up state -> `idle`
- local lint/format checks passed on the touched files

## Notes

A focused local Vitest run hit a Node internal assertion in this environment, so the behavior was additionally checked with a direct harness against the patched TUI logic.

target.title: Plugins: expose structured finalLlmOutcome on agent_end
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `agent_end` only exposed `messages`, `success`, `error`, and `durationMs`, so terminal provider failures could still look successful to plugins when they surfaced as a final assistant `stopReason: "error"` turn.
- Why it matters: downstream plugins need a structured signal to distinguish provider, prompt, and runner failures and to react to terminal auth failures like 401 Unauthorized.
- What changed: added optional `finalLlmOutcome` to `PluginHookAgentEndEvent`, populated it in the embedded runner from prompt-build failures, thrown provider errors with structured HTTP status, and terminal assistant failure turns, and documented/tested the new field.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): existing `agent_end` fields remain intact, `success` semantics stay unchanged, and this PR does not redesign hooks or change `error` into an object.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

### Expected

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commit `2c5071b11`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/plugins/types.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`, `docs/tools/plugin.md`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: plugins unexpectedly assuming `finalLlmOutcome.statusCode` is always present, or treating `success` and `finalLlmOutcome.ok` as equivalent.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: some provider failures still arrive only as terminal assistant error text after the upstream SDK flattens the original exception, so structured HTTP status cannot always be preserved.
  - Mitigation: `finalLlmOutcome` still marks those runs as `ok: false` with `source: "provider"`, and `statusCode` is populated only when OpenClaw has structured status data.

target.title: fix(ui): keep delete confirm in viewport
target.body:
## Related issue

Related #50277

## Summary

- Position the chat delete confirmation popover relative to the viewport instead of the scroll container.
- Choose above or below the delete button based on available vertical space.
- Clamp the popover horizontally so it remains reachable on narrow viewports.
- Add focused jsdom geometry coverage for top-edge, bottom-edge, and narrow-width placement.

## User-visible behavior

The delete confirmation popover in Control UI chat should remain visible and reachable when deleting messages near the top or edges of the chat viewport.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI grouped chat delete confirmation could render out of reach when opened near the top or horizontal viewport edges because it was absolutely positioned inside the scrollable chat area. This patch positions the popover against the viewport, flips it above or below the delete button, and clamps it inside viewport margins.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `4a0465007c` on Raspberry Pi-class Linux host, Node `v22.21.1`, Chromium `142.0.7444.175`, using the PR's `.chat-delete-confirm` markup/CSS and viewport-placement code path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
chromium --headless=new --disable-gpu --no-sandbox --force-device-scale-factor=1 --window-size=360,640 --dump-dom file:///tmp/openclaw-delete-confirm-proof.html
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal output excerpt from Chromium:

```text
OpenClaw Control UI delete-confirm viewport proof
viewport=500x553
top-edge: placement=below left=90 top=38 withinViewport=true
bottom-edge: placement=above left=12 top=86 withinViewport=true
horizontal-edge: placement=below left=104 top=130 withinViewport=true
```

- Observed result after fix: In Chromium, the delete confirmation popover remains inside the viewport margins for top-edge, bottom-edge, and horizontal-edge cases. The top-edge case opens below the trigger, the bottom-edge case opens above the trigger, and the horizontal-edge case stays within the right boundary.
- What was not tested: Full gateway-backed dashboard click path on this Raspberry Pi-class host; dependency installation was intentionally avoided. Visual screenshot capture was not attached because copied browser output is the supplied proof.

## Supplemental validation

- `git diff --check` exited 0 locally with no whitespace errors.
- GitHub Actions `checks-node-core-ui` passed on head `4a0465007c`.
- GitHub Actions `check-test-types` passed on head `4a0465007c` after adding `this: HTMLElement` to the test geometry mock.

## AI-assisted

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the diff and understand the change scope.

target.title: test(gateway): consolidate deriveSessionTitle tests into dedicated module
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes an issue where heartbeat polls could overwrite explicit user-labeled session titles with transcript-derived titles like `"heartbeat"`.

## Changes

- Extracts `deriveSessionTitle` to `session-title.ts` with a dedicated test module.
- Preserves explicit session labels before transcript-derived fallbacks.
- Keeps auto-populated `origin.label` behind `firstUserMessage` so unlabeled inbound DMs can still use transcript-derived titles.
- Keeps `session-utils.ts` as a compatibility re-export site.
- Clarifies the zero timestamp test name to describe the actual `updatedAt: 0` input.

## Title precedence

1. `displayName`
2. `subject`
3. explicit `entry.label`
4. `firstUserMessage` (truncated)
5. auto-populated `entry.origin?.label`
6. `sessionId` prefix fallback

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: explicit session labels should survive heartbeat-style transcript summaries, while auto-populated origin labels should not override a real first user message.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on this branch after rebasing onto current `main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --eval 'import { deriveSessionTitle } from "./src/gateway/session-title.ts";
const cases = {
  explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), label: "My App - Dashboard" }, "heartbeat"),
  transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, "Need help with deployment"),
  originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, null),
  zeroUpdatedAtFallback: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abcd1234-5678-90ef-ghij-klmnopqrstuv", updatedAt: 0 }),
};
console.log(JSON.stringify(cases, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat": "My App - Dashboard",
  "transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel": "Need help with deployment",
  "originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript": "Discord: Engineering",
  "zeroUpdatedAtFallback": "abcd1234"
}
```

Observed result after fix: explicit labels win over heartbeat transcript text; transcript text wins over auto-populated origin labels; origin labels still act as fallback when there is no transcript; zero `updatedAt` keeps the session-id-only fallback.

What was not tested: full gateway UI rendering was not manually opened; this proof covers the extracted title derivation behavior directly.

## Validation

- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-title.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

target.title: feat(telegram): add guest mode
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not handle Telegram Guest Bot `guest_message` updates, so bots could not answer `@bot` guest queries from groups where the bot is not a member. - Why it matters: Guest Bots are a Telegram-supported way to let a bot answer from outside a group without joining it, but they require a different update type and response API than normal group messages. - What changed: Telegram now supports opt-in `guest_message` handling, routes allowed guest queries through an isolated direct-style agent turn, preserves sender metadata, and delivers exactly one visible response through `answerGuestQuery`. OpenClaw only requests `guest_message` updates when Guest Mode is explicitly enabled, or when opt-in `"auto"` mode sees Telegram bot metadata confirming guest-query support. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal Telegram group/direct message handling remains on the existing send path. Guest Mode is disabled by default and does not inherit per-group policies or normal group conversation bindings; it uses account-level Telegram allowlist rules. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #79077 - Partially addresses #79077 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram bots can answer Guest Bot queries from group chats where the bot is not a member when `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true` is configured, or when opt-in `"auto"` mode is configured and startup Telegram bot metadata reports guest-query support. - Real environment tested: Ubuntu Linux, local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway, Telegram channel configured, OpenAI Codex with GPT-5.5. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Activate the guest mode in the BotFather telegram bot settings. 2. Set `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Built OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Built Control UI assets. 5. Restarted the local gateway. 6. Sent a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot was not a member. (`@openclawbot what time is it, can you read previous messages here? What is my telegram id, what is this channel / group id?`) 7. Verified that one visible answer appeared in Telegram. 8. Verified that users not allowed by the account-level allowlist could not use Guest Mode. 9. Tested groups where the bot is already a member to check that normal Telegram group handling did not regress. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Observed result after fix: The guest query created an isolated direct-style Telegram guest session and produced one visible Telegram answer. Unauthorized guest senders were blocked by account-level allowFrom. Normal groups where the bot is already a member continued to work through the existing Telegram message path. - What was not tested: Bot-to-bot mode, long-running multi-account (more than 3) production soak testing, and cross-platform release-package install proof. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, OpenClaw did not include guest_message in Telegram allowed updates and had no answerGuestQuery delivery path, so Guest Bot queries were not converted into agent turns. (So this did just not exist previously) ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: N/A for a new feature. Telegram Guest Bots use a new update and response shape that the existing Telegram channel did not support. - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Telegram tests covered normal message updates, but not guest_message allowed updates, guest session isolation, or answerGuestQuery delivery. - Contributing context (if known): Guest Mode is similar to inline behavior from a product perspective, but Telegram delivers it as a guest message that must be answered through the Bot API guest-answer endpoint rather than the normal send-message path. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts - Scenario the test should lock in: A guest update is accepted, routed to an isolated guest session, keeps sender metadata available to the agent, does not inherit normal group bindings or group policies, and sends only one visible Telegram guest answer. Guest updates are only requested when Guest Mode is enabled/supported, and guest update queueing is keyed by source group + sender instead of the normal group lane. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the Telegram plugin boundary where allowed updates, bot capability detection, guest routing, session context, authorization, and delivery behavior meet, without requiring live Telegram credentials. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. New tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled`. - omitted / `false`: ignore guest queries and do not request `guest_message` updates from Telegram. - `true`: request and handle Telegram guest queries. - `"auto"`: request and handle guest queries only when startup Telegram bot metadata reports guest-query support. Guest Mode uses the Telegram account-level allowlist. It intentionally does not apply per-group allowlists or normal group conversation bindings because the bot is not a member of the source group in the guest interaction. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [Telegram guest_message] -> [not in allowed updates / no guest delivery path] -> [no visible answer] After: [Telegram guest_message] -> [account-level allowlist] -> [isolated direct-style guest session] -> [agent turn] -> [answerGuestQuery] -> [one visible Telegram answer] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No. - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) Yes. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No. - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No. - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a Telegram guest-response capability using Telegram Bot API answerGuestQuery. Guest access is gated by the existing Telegram account-level allowFrom list, and OpenClaw only requests guest_message updates when Guest Mode is enabled or explicitly configured for supported auto handling. Guest turns use isolated direct-style session keys, skip normal group bindings, and deliver only one visible response. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu Linux. - Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway. - Model/provider: OpenAI Codex, GPT-5.5. - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram. - Relevant config (redacted): Telegram channel configured with `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`; bot token and account details redacted. ### Steps 1. Enable guest mode for the bot using the telegram BotFather. 2. Configure `channels.telegram.guest.enabled: true`. 3. Build OpenClaw from this branch. 4. Build Control UI assets. 5. Restart the local gateway. 6. Send a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot is not a member. 7. Confirm that Telegram shows one visible bot answer. 8. Send a guest query from a user that is not allowed by account-level allowFrom. 9. Send normal messages in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Expected - Allowed guest queries create an isolated guest session and produce one visible Telegram answer. - Unauthorized guest senders are blocked. - Normal Telegram group handling still works in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Actual - Allowed guest queries produced one visible answer. - Unauthorized guest senders were blocked. - Existing normal Telegram group handling still worked. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [X] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live Telegram Guest Mode round trip from a group where the bot was not a member. - Unauthorized guest sender blocked by account-level allowlist. - Normal Telegram groups where the bot is already a member still handled messages normally. - Control UI assets load after pnpm ui:build. - Edge cases checked: - Unauthorized guest sender fallback. - Guest isolation from normal group bindings. (Needs to remove bot in between from the group) - What you did not verify: - Bot-to-bot mode. - Long-running multi-account (>3) production soak. - Cross-platform release-package install proof. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. Local Codex review was run during the beta-branch iteration and again after moving the patch onto current `origin/main`. Actionable findings from the earlier rounds were addressed before opening the PR. The final main-based Codex review reported no discrete actionable correctness issues. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes. - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) Yes. - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Guest Mode could accidentally inherit normal group routing state. - Mitigation: Guest turns use isolated direct-style guest session keys and skip normal conversation bindings. - Risk: Telegram Guest Mode supports only one visible answer for this interaction shape. - Mitigation: Guest delivery sends only the first final answer through answerGuestQuery. - Risk: Unauthorized users could trigger guest turns. - Mitigation: Guest authorization uses the Telegram account-level allowFrom list. ~Horo

target.title: fix(migrate): use resolved provider for options
target.body:
## Summary
- build migration provider options from the resolved provider id
- pass the resolved provider id through migration apply preflight and apply contexts
- add provider-options coverage for defaulted Codex migrations with Codex-only options

Fixes #83285.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 83323 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/pr-83323-fix-migrate-use-resolved-provider-for-options-before-after-proof.png)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/after-pr-83323.log

Behavior addressed: Codex-specific migration options should be included even when the provider defaults to Codex instead of being explicitly supplied in raw CLI opts.
Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `02020a4098` on branch `tmp-pr-83323-proof`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts src/commands/migrate/apply.test.ts --reporter=dot`.
Evidence after fix:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /root/src/openclaw-branches/fix-migrate-provider-options-83285

✓ commands  ../../src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts (2 tests)\n✓ commands  ../../src/commands/migrate/apply.test.ts (1 test)

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  2 passed (2)

[check:changed] src/commands/migrate/apply.ts: core production
[check:changed] src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts: core test
[check:changed] src/commands/migrate/providers.ts: core production
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

Observed result after fix: `buildMigrationProviderOptions({ configPatchMode: "return", verifyPluginApps: true }, "codex")` returns both Codex-only options even when `opts.provider` is omitted, and `runMigrationApply` forwards those options into both the preflight `plan` context and final `apply` context.
What was not tested: a live migration against an actual Codex workspace; this proof exercises the embedded/defaulted apply path with a real `runMigrationApply` call and a test migration provider.
- Before evidence (terminal/code capture from `origin/main`):

```text
src/commands/migrate/providers.ts before this patch checked only raw opts.provider:
  if (opts.provider === "codex" && opts.verifyPluginApps === true) { ... }
  if (opts.provider === "codex" && opts.configPatchMode) { ... }

src/commands/migrate/apply.ts called:
  providerOptions: buildMigrationProviderOptions(params.opts)

When the apply path had resolved providerId="codex" but opts.provider was undefined, Codex-only providerOptions were omitted.
```

## Root Cause
- Root cause: provider option construction used the raw optional CLI provider field rather than the provider id already resolved by the migrate command.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no focused coverage for defaulted Codex migrations carrying Codex-only options.
- Contributing context: `createMigrationPlan` requires `opts.provider`, but `runMigrationApply` receives `providerId` separately while `opts.provider` can remain undefined.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this: unit test.
- Target tests or files: `src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts`, `src/commands/migrate/apply.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Codex-only provider options are built from the resolved provider id, omitted for other providers, and forwarded into the apply path when raw `opts.provider` is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to provider option construction and apply-path plumbing.

## Validation
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
target.body:
# Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks

Branch: `policy-conformance-examples`
GitHub base: `main`
Logical base: `policy-doctor-consumer`
Current draft: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80783
Draft status: combined final policy example PR

## Title

Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks

## Summary

This PR extends the bundled `policy` extension with three additional
conformance areas: model providers, private-network access settings, and
OpenClaw-managed MCP servers.

The goal is to show that policy is not a second governance system. It is a
thin conformance layer over existing OpenClaw settings:

- `policy.jsonc` defines authored requirements.
- OpenClaw config remains the source of truth for workspace state.
- Policy extracts evidence from existing settings.
- The policy extension registers health checks for concrete authored rules.
- `doctor --lint` reports drift.
- `policy check --json` emits the recordable audit tuple:
  `policyHash + evidenceHash + findingsHash + clean result -> attestationHash`.
  `checkedAt` remains audit metadata in the JSON output, not an input to the
  stable attestation hash.

This follows the channels and tools policy slices, but keeps the added areas
read-only. It proves the shape works across several OpenClaw settings
categories without adding runtime enforcement or duplicate registries.

## Motivation

Enterprise operators need to prove workspaces conform to expected standards
and approval processes. That is broader than one setting family:

- model provider choices may need to stay inside an approved set;
- private-network browser/fetch access may need to stay disabled;
- MCP server entries may need to be limited to approved workspace servers.

These are all policy health checks: they compare authored requirements against
observed OpenClaw state and produce findings when the workspace drifts.

## Maintainer Input Requested

1. **Is this the right policy shape for multiple settings areas?**

   This PR keeps policy as a conformance layer over existing OpenClaw config,
   not as a duplicate model registry, network system, or MCP registry. Is this
   the right long-term shape?

2. **Are these evidence boundaries correct?**

   The draft observes:

   - `models.providers.*` and selected agent model refs;
   - existing browser and web-fetch SSRF private-network settings;
   - OpenClaw-managed `mcp.servers.*` entries.

   Are those the right first boundaries for these policy areas?

3. **Should these remain read-only?**

   This PR intentionally reports non-conformance only. It does not remove MCP
   servers, rewrite model config, or change network settings. Should repairs
   for these areas remain explicit follow-ups?

## What Changed

- Added model-provider evidence to `policy check --json`:
  `modelProviders` and `modelRefs`.
- Added `models.providers.allow` and `models.providers.deny` policy support.
- Added `policy/models-denied-provider`.
- Added `policy/models-unapproved-provider`.
- Added network evidence to `policy check --json`.
- Added `network.privateNetwork.allow`.
- Added `policy/network-private-access-enabled`.
- Added MCP server evidence to `policy check --json`.
- Added `mcp.servers.allow` and `mcp.servers.deny` policy support.
- Added `policy/mcp-denied-server`.
- Added `policy/mcp-unapproved-server`.
- Updated policy CLI and plugin reference docs.
- Added tests for denied rules, allowlists, evidence extraction, and empty
  category namespaces.

## Policy Shape

```jsonc
{
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "allow": ["openai", "anthropic"],
      "deny": ["openrouter"],
    },
  },
  "network": {
    "privateNetwork": {
      "allow": false,
    },
  },
  "mcp": {
    "servers": {
      "allow": ["docs"],
      "deny": ["untrusted"],
    },
  },
}
```

Category blocks are namespaces. A block such as `models: {}`, `network: {}`,
or `mcp: {}` does not run a check by itself. A check runs when a concrete
authored requirement is present, such as `models.providers.allow`,
`network.privateNetwork.allow`, or `mcp.servers.deny`.

Deny wins over allow. If a provider or server is denied, the finding reports
the deny rule and does not also report the allowlist rule for the same target.

## Evidence Shape

```json
{
  "evidence": {
    "modelProviders": [
      {
        "id": "openai",
        "source": "oc://openclaw.config/models/providers/openai"
      }
    ],
    "modelRefs": [
      {
        "ref": "openai/gpt-5.5",
        "provider": "openai",
        "model": "gpt-5.5",
        "source": "oc://openclaw.config/agents/defaults/model"
      }
    ],
    "network": [
      {
        "id": "browser-private-network",
        "source": "oc://openclaw.config/browser/ssrfPolicy/dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork",
        "value": false
      }
    ],
    "mcpServers": [
      {
        "id": "docs",
        "transport": "stdio",
        "source": "oc://openclaw.config/mcp/servers/docs",
        "command": "npx"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Safety

`doctor --lint` and `policy check` are read-only for these areas. This PR does
not:

- change model config or intercept model runtime calls;
- change browser or web-fetch network config;
- edit `mcp.servers`, start MCP runtimes, stop MCP runtimes, or contact remote
  MCP servers.

Findings identify both sides of the decision:

- `target` points to the observed OpenClaw setting.
- `requirement` points to the authored policy rule.

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed**: Enabled bundled policy registers model-provider, MCP-server, and private-network health checks, collects config-derived evidence, and emits structured findings with `oc://` target/requirement paths when configured model providers, MCP servers, or private-network SSRF posture violates policy.

**Real environment tested**: Clean WSL detached worktrees for upstream/main and PR #80783 head, with a temporary OpenClaw workspace containing enabled policy config, `policy.jsonc`, and model/MCP/network config inputs.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

1. Created a clean upstream/main worktree and a clean PR-head worktree at #80783 head.
2. In each worktree, ran `node --import ./node_modules/tsx/dist/loader.mjs probe-policy-conformance-examples.mjs` against a temp workspace.
3. Authored `policy.jsonc` with model provider `deny`/`allow`, MCP server `deny`/`allow`, and `network.privateNetwork.allow=false` requirements.
4. Supplied OpenClaw config containing denied/unapproved model providers and refs, denied/unapproved MCP servers, and private-network SSRF settings.
5. Ran the registered policy doctor checks through `runDoctorLintChecks()` and captured `evaluatePolicy()` evidence.

**Evidence before fix**: Upstream/main has no bundled policy extension, no policy doctor registration, no model/MCP/network policy check IDs, no config-derived model/MCP/network evidence, and no findings for those categories.

**Evidence after fix**:

![Fresh before/after proof for model, MCP, and network policy checks](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80783-fresh/pr-80783/pr-80783-policy-model-mcp-network-conformance-before-after-proof.png)

**Observed result after fix**: PR #80783 registers the five new category checks (`policy/mcp-denied-server`, `policy/mcp-unapproved-server`, `policy/models-denied-provider`, `policy/models-unapproved-provider`, and `policy/network-private-access-enabled`), runs 15 policy checks, records model provider/ref evidence, MCP server evidence, and private-network evidence, and emits 8 structured findings with `oc://openclaw.config/...` targets and matching `oc://policy.jsonc/...` requirements.

**What was not tested**: This proof does not broaden the draft beyond the example categories; full finding/evidence records are preserved in the artifact branch JSON files.

## Testing

```bash
node scripts/test-extension.mjs policy --reporter=dot
pnpm oxlint extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/openclaw.plugin.json
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md
pnpm docs:check-mdx -- docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md
```

Latest local result:

- Policy extension tests: 3 files, 33 tests passed.
- Focused oxlint: 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`: passed.
- Focused docs formatting: passed.
- Focused docs MDX: passed.

## Diff Size

Against the logical base `policy-doctor-consumer`:

- 7 files changed
- 1071 insertions
- 29 deletions

## Related

- Doctor health contract and lint validation:
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80055
- Channels-first policy conformance:
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80407
- Tool metadata conformance and runtime trusted tool policy:
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80056
- Runtime audit metadata and attestation enforcement:
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81104
<!-- policy-stack-links:start -->

## Policy stack links

This PR is part of the Policy 1.0 proof stack. Final category branches added after the runtime-audit layer:

- #81974 — Policy: add secrets auth provenance checks
- #81981 — Policy: add gateway exposure checks

Full stack reference:

1. #80407 — Policy: add conformance system with channel checks
2. #80056 — Policy: add tool metadata conformance and runtime checks
3. #80783 — Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
4. #81104 — Policy: add runtime audit metadata and attestation enforcement
5. #81974 — Policy: add secrets auth provenance checks
6. #81981 — Policy: add gateway exposure checks

<!-- policy-stack-links:end -->

target.title: fix(doctor): preserve unknown web search records
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve unrelated record-valued `tools.web.search` entries during legacy web search migration
- align `tools.web.search` validation with its extensible config type so doctor writes do not drop extension-owned settings, while still rejecting known legacy provider-scoped records
- continue removing only positively identified legacy provider records and dangerous prototype keys
- add regression coverage for custom record-valued web search config next to a legacy `apiKey`

Fixes #83287.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: legacy web search migration should move known provider-owned settings while preserving unrelated extension-owned `tools.web.search` records.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `154550b971d0` on branch `tmp-pr-83315-proof`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /root/src/openclaw-branches/base
git fetch origin pull/83315/head:tmp-pr-83315-proof --force
git switch tmp-pr-83315-proof
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$cfg" node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --fix --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
before tools.web.search:
{
  "apiKey": "brave-key",
  "customSearch": {
    "endpoint": "https://search.example.test",
    "mode": "strict"
  },
  "openaiCodex": {
    "enabled": true
  }
}
command: OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-83315-config.NyMROQ.json node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --fix --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions
Doctor changes:
  Moved tools.web.search.apiKey → plugins.entries.brave.config.webSearch.apiKey.
Updated config: /tmp/openclaw-83315-config.NyMROQ.json

after tools.web.search and migrated plugin config:
{
  "customSearch": {
    "endpoint": "https://search.example.test",
    "mode": "strict"
  },
  "openaiCodex": {
    "enabled": true
  }
}
plugins.entries.brave.config.webSearch = {"apiKey":"brave-key"}
```

Observed result after fix: `doctor --fix` migrated the legacy global search key to `plugins.entries.brave.config.webSearch.apiKey`, removed `tools.web.search.apiKey`, and preserved both `tools.web.search.customSearch` and `tools.web.search.openaiCodex` in the written config.

What was not tested: a live web search call using the preserved custom provider record; this proof covers the on-disk doctor migration/write path.

## Root Cause
- Root cause: normalization treated all record-valued unknown keys as removable unless they were in a small modern allowlist, and schema validation was stricter than the config type.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test covered an unrelated record-valued key beside a migrated legacy provider key through config validation, or the schema boundary that keeps known legacy provider records invalid.
- Contributing context: the migration needs to remove legacy provider records, but that removal condition was broader than the set of known legacy providers.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this: unit plus config validation test.
- Target tests or files: `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-web-search-migrate.test.ts`, `src/config/web-search-codex-config.test.ts`, `src/config/config.web-search-provider.test.ts`.
- Scenario the tests should lock in: known legacy provider config is migrated/rejected while unrelated record-valued config remains under `tools.web.search` and survives validation.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to config normalization and schema validation before broader doctor plumbing.

## Validation
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/config.web-search-provider.test.ts src/config/web-search-codex-config.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-web-search-migrate.test.ts --reporter=dot` in WSL: 3 files, 41 tests passed.
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js --noEmit --pretty false -p tsconfig.json` in WSL.
- `git diff --check`.

target.title: fix(feishu): send p2p replies to user targets
target.body:
Fixes #83730.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Feishu p2p final replies were being dispatched to the raw p2p `chat_id` (`oc_*`) after reply-to delivery is skipped for DMs.
- The inbound path already computes the safer sender target as `user:<open_id>`, so this sends p2p final delivery through that target while preserving group/topic delivery through `chat:<chat_id>`.

Real environment tested:
- Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS.
- Production Feishu message routing path exercised with Vitest using the real `handleFeishuMessage` dispatcher setup.
- No live Feishu tenant credentials were used; the proof validates the target passed into the Feishu send dispatcher immediately before network delivery.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts -t "routes p2p final delivery"
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 69 skipped (70)
```

The focused regression asserts:
```text
p2p dispatcher chatId = user:ou_sender_1
p2p replyToMessageId = msg-p2p-target
p2p skipReplyToInMessages = true
```

Additional target mapping proof:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node --import tsx -e 'import { resolveReceiveIdType } from "./extensions/feishu/src/targets.ts"; console.log(JSON.stringify({ dmTarget: "user:ou_sender_1", dmReceiveIdType: resolveReceiveIdType("user:ou_sender_1"), groupTarget: "chat:oc-group", groupReceiveIdType: resolveReceiveIdType("chat:oc-group") }, null, 2));'
{
  "dmTarget": "user:ou_sender_1",
  "dmReceiveIdType": "open_id",
  "groupTarget": "chat:oc-group",
  "groupReceiveIdType": "chat_id"
}
```

Observed result after fix:
- p2p final reply dispatch targets `user:ou_sender_1`, which maps to Feishu `open_id` delivery.
- p2p reply-to suppression is preserved (`skipReplyToInMessages=true`) so the fix does not re-enable the failing direct reply-to path.
- Existing group/topic behavior remains on `chat:oc-group`, mapping to Feishu `chat_id`.

Full validation:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts

Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  179 passed (179)
```

Other checks:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts
git diff --check
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```

Commit author verification:
```console
d5c375b96f Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> fix(feishu): send p2p replies to user targets
```

What was not tested:
- Live Feishu API delivery against a real tenant, because tenant credentials were not available in this environment.

target.title: fix(update): repair managed npm plugin peers
target.body:
## Summary
- recover managed npm install records from the actual managed package when a persisted npm record points at the same path but an older version
- relink managed npm `openclaw` peer dependencies during post-core convergence before payload smoke checks run
- validate `openclaw` peer links with the existing host-root realpath audit semantics, including stale real directory and wrong-target regressions

## Review follow-up
- Addressed ClawSweeper P2 by exporting and reusing `auditOpenClawPeerDependencyLink` from `plugin-peer-link.ts` in the payload smoke check.
- The passing peer-link smoke test now requires a symlink that resolves to the current host package root.
- Added regressions for a stale real `node_modules/openclaw` directory and a symlink pointing at the wrong package root.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Managed npm plugins that declare `peerDependencies.openclaw` can fail after an upgrade when the plugin-local `node_modules/openclaw` host-package link is missing, stale, or pointed at the wrong host root. This proof covers stale install-record recovery, before/after peer repair, and the stricter stale-directory guard.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on the rebased PR branch, running against the real source modules with `pnpm exec tsx`. The managed npm root was sandboxed under a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`; private local filesystem paths are redacted as `[TMP_OPENCLAW_STATE]`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm exec tsx --tsconfig tsconfig.json <inline managed-npm peer repair proof>`. The script created a sandboxed managed npm package declaring `peerDependencies.openclaw`, seeded a stale persisted install record (`2026.5.16-beta.1`), loaded recovered install records, ran `runPluginPayloadSmokeCheck` before repair, ran `relinkOpenClawPeerDependenciesInManagedNpmRoot`, ran `runPluginPayloadSmokeCheck` after repair, audited the resulting peer link, and separately checked a stale real-directory collision.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the after-fix proof run:

```text
REAL BEHAVIOR PROOF: managed npm OpenClaw peer repair
stateDir=[TMP_OPENCLAW_STATE]
managedPackage=@proof/openclaw-peer-plugin
flow=relinkOpenClawPeerDependenciesInManagedNpmRoot (doctor/post-core repair primitive)
persistedVersion=2026.5.16-beta.1
recoveredVersion=2026.5.18-beta.1
recoveredSpec=@proof/openclaw-peer-plugin@2026.5.18-beta.1
staleIntegrityCleared=true
beforeSmokeFailures=1
beforeSmoke=[{"reason":"missing-openclaw-peer-link","detailHasMissingPeer":true,"detailHasWrongTarget":false}]
repairResult={"checked":1,"attempted":1,"repaired":1,"skipped":0}
installRecordRetained=true
afterSmokeFailures=0
afterPeerAudit=ok
staleRealDirectoryFailures=1
staleRealDirectorySmoke=[{"reason":"missing-openclaw-peer-link","detailHasMissingPeer":false,"detailHasWrongTarget":true}]
```

**Observed result after fix:** The stale persisted install record was recovered to `2026.5.18-beta.1` and stale integrity metadata was dropped. Before repair, payload smoke found one `missing-openclaw-peer-link` failure. The managed npm relinker checked one package, attempted one repair, repaired one link, and skipped zero. After repair, payload smoke reported zero failures and the peer audit returned `ok`. A stale real `node_modules/openclaw` directory still failed smoke validation with the wrong-target signal.

**What was not tested:** I did not mutate Jay's live `~/.openclaw` runtime or run a production networked `openclaw update`; the proof used an isolated managed npm state so the live gateway/config/secrets were not touched.

## Testing
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-records.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-peer-link.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/plugin-payload-validation.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/plugins/plugin-peer-link.ts src/cli/update-cli/plugin-payload-validation.ts src/cli/update-cli/plugin-payload-validation.test.ts src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.ts src/cli/update-cli/post-core-plugin-convergence.test.ts src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-record-reader.ts src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-records.test.ts`

target.title: fix(agents): preserve inbound context in runtime-only prompt
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #83767.

In `runEmbeddedAttempt`, channel-level `currentInboundContext` (Telegram/Discord reply target body, mention metadata, room-event context) was unconditionally dropped from the model-visible prompt whenever `promptSubmission.runtimeOnly` was true.

Only `runtimeContext` (plugin/hook-derived, hidden) was supposed to be relocated to the system prompt in that branch — but the same conditional swept up channel inbound context as well. Result: a user replying to an earlier message and mentioning the bot got an answer with no awareness of the replied-to message body.

## Change

`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`:

```diff
 const promptForModel = buildCurrentInboundPrompt({
-  context: promptSubmission.runtimeOnly ? undefined : params.currentInboundContext,
+  context: params.currentInboundContext,
   prompt: promptSubmission.prompt,
 });
```

The existing runtime-system-context branch immediately below still handles relocation of plugin/hook context to the system prompt, so hidden runtime context does not leak into the transcript.

## Regression test

Adds a test in `attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts` exercising the exact gap identified in #83767: a runtime-only turn (`transcriptPrompt: ""`) with non-empty `currentInboundContext` carrying a reply target. The test asserts the reply-target block reaches the model prompt alongside the runtime-event marker.

This complements existing coverage:
- "submits runtime-only context through system prompt without visible prompt" (no inbound context)
- "submits suppressed room event context as the model prompt" (room event with `suppressNextUserMessagePersistence: true`, which is the `model-prompt` mode, not runtime-only)

The previous suite had no scenario where both `runtimeOnly: true` and `currentInboundContext` were present.

## Acceptance criteria (per issue review)

- [ ] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`
- [ ] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts`
- [ ] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`

(Local validation requires workspace setup; relying on CI.)

## Versions affected

- Last good: 2026.5.12
- Broken: 2026.5.18 (commit `50a2481`)
- Originating commit (per issue review): `1912be8619fb`

target.title: fix(doctor): preserve unknown web search records
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve unrelated record-valued `tools.web.search` entries during legacy web search migration
- align `tools.web.search` validation with its extensible config type so doctor writes do not drop extension-owned settings, while still rejecting known legacy provider-scoped records
- continue removing only positively identified legacy provider records and dangerous prototype keys
- add regression coverage for custom record-valued web search config next to a legacy `apiKey`

Fixes #83287.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: legacy web search migration should move known provider-owned settings while preserving unrelated extension-owned `tools.web.search` records.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `154550b971d0` on branch `tmp-pr-83315-proof`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /root/src/openclaw-branches/base
git fetch origin pull/83315/head:tmp-pr-83315-proof --force
git switch tmp-pr-83315-proof
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$cfg" node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --fix --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
before tools.web.search:
{
  "apiKey": "brave-key",
  "customSearch": {
    "endpoint": "https://search.example.test",
    "mode": "strict"
  },
  "openaiCodex": {
    "enabled": true
  }
}
command: OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-83315-config.NyMROQ.json node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --fix --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions
Doctor changes:
  Moved tools.web.search.apiKey → plugins.entries.brave.config.webSearch.apiKey.
Updated config: /tmp/openclaw-83315-config.NyMROQ.json

after tools.web.search and migrated plugin config:
{
  "customSearch": {
    "endpoint": "https://search.example.test",
    "mode": "strict"
  },
  "openaiCodex": {
    "enabled": true
  }
}
plugins.entries.brave.config.webSearch = {"apiKey":"brave-key"}
```

Observed result after fix: `doctor --fix` migrated the legacy global search key to `plugins.entries.brave.config.webSearch.apiKey`, removed `tools.web.search.apiKey`, and preserved both `tools.web.search.customSearch` and `tools.web.search.openaiCodex` in the written config.

What was not tested: a live web search call using the preserved custom provider record; this proof covers the on-disk doctor migration/write path.

## Root Cause
- Root cause: normalization treated all record-valued unknown keys as removable unless they were in a small modern allowlist, and schema validation was stricter than the config type.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test covered an unrelated record-valued key beside a migrated legacy provider key through config validation, or the schema boundary that keeps known legacy provider records invalid.
- Contributing context: the migration needs to remove legacy provider records, but that removal condition was broader than the set of known legacy providers.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this: unit plus config validation test.
- Target tests or files: `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-web-search-migrate.test.ts`, `src/config/web-search-codex-config.test.ts`, `src/config/config.web-search-provider.test.ts`.
- Scenario the tests should lock in: known legacy provider config is migrated/rejected while unrelated record-valued config remains under `tools.web.search` and survives validation.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to config normalization and schema validation before broader doctor plumbing.

## Validation
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/config.web-search-provider.test.ts src/config/web-search-codex-config.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-web-search-migrate.test.ts --reporter=dot` in WSL: 3 files, 41 tests passed.
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js --noEmit --pretty false -p tsconfig.json` in WSL.
- `git diff --check`.

target.title: fix(exec): block configured denied path operands
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #11829 calls out agent access to provider/API key material as a security roadmap gap, and the issue discussion proposed a configurable `tools.exec.deniedPaths` surface for tenant-controlled secret paths.
- Solution: add `tools.exec.deniedPaths` as an exec preflight guard that rejects commands referencing configured paths such as `/run/secrets/**` before gateway/node/sandbox execution. The guard now inspects command-carrier and shell-wrapper payloads, sandbox checks resolve relative operands in the container path namespace, node-host checks run after node target resolution using the target node platform namespace, config loading preserves authored denied-path `~` patterns until execution, home-relative denied patterns such as `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` resolve against the selected exec HOME, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands are expanded before denied-path matching.
- What changed: runtime guard, global/agent config wiring, zod/type/schema help/labels/tags, docs, generated config baseline, config path normalization, and regression tests for direct paths, shell-wrapper payloads, command substitutions, sandbox container-relative traversal, node-host Windows path semantics, home-relative denied patterns, and HOME-prefixed command operands.
- What did NOT change: no changes to model catalog filtering, auth proxy docs, or the existing models.json key-stripping work already covered by other PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #11829
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- This gives operators a config-level guard for mounted secret directories, especially deployments that keep provider credentials in predictable paths such as `/run/secrets/**`. It is defense-in-depth rather than a replacement for sandboxing or secret isolation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: exec commands can currently reference mounted sensitive paths unless deployment isolation catches the access; after this patch a configured denied path is rejected before host execution.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node 22.22.0, invoking the real `createExecTool` runtime with `host: "gateway"`, `security: "full"`, `ask: "off"`, and `deniedPaths: ["/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/**"]`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` importing `./src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`, creating the exec tool, and calling `tool.execute(...)` for these commands:
  - `cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"`
  - `bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'`
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'`
- Evidence after fix (copied live local runtime output):

```text
blocked: cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
```

- Observed result after fix: the exec tool throws before running each command, including the shell-wrapper and command-substitution forms, so the denied path is not handed to gateway execution.
- Additional sandbox namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` now covers `host: "sandbox"`, `workdir: "/workspace"`, `deniedPaths: ["/run/secrets/**"]`, and `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key`. The preflight resolves the operand against `/workspace` using POSIX container semantics and rejects `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec` is called.
- Additional node namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` now covers `host: "node"` after a Windows node target resolves with `platform: "win32"`, `workdir: "C:\\Work"`, `deniedPaths: ["C:\\Secrets\\**"]`, and `type C:\\Secrets\\provider.key`. The node path preflight rejects the command before `system.run` is called. It also covers case-insensitive Windows matching with `type c:\\secrets\\provider.key`.
- Additional home-relative proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` covers `deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"]` and blocks commands referencing the resolved HOME credential path via literal resolved paths, `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`, and a shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` payload. `src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` verifies authored `tools.exec.deniedPaths` entries stay home-relative during generic config path normalization so the exec guard can resolve them later in the selected runtime namespace.
- Additional config-through-runtime namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` flows `normalizeConfigPaths({ tools: { exec: { deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"] } } })` into sandbox exec with `HOME=/workspace` and proves `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` is blocked as `/workspace/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`. `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` flows the same normalized config into a Windows node target with `HOME=C:\\Users\\agent` and proves `C:\\Users\\agent\\.openclaw\\credentials\\provider.key` is blocked before `system.run`.
- What was not tested: a full multi-tenant Docker deployment with real mounted provider secrets.
- Before evidence: the new regression tests failed before the follow-up fixes because `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'`, `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'`, the sandbox container-relative traversal, the Windows node target command, the home-relative denied pattern, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands all resolved successfully with `(no output)` or left path prefixes unexpanded instead of rejecting.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: exec had approval/safe-bin/env guards, but no config-level path denylist for sensitive operand paths. The first implementation scanned only the outer argv tokens, used host path semantics for sandbox relative operands, checked node targets before the target platform was known, let generic config normalization consume authored home-relative denied patterns before runtime namespace selection, did not normalize home-relative denied patterns, and did not expand common `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands before matching.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test asserted that a configured secret path is blocked inside a shell wrapper, command substitution, sandbox container-relative path, Windows node target path, `~/...` denied pattern, or HOME-prefixed command operand before execution.
- Contributing context (if known): issue #11829 covers a broader roadmap; this PR implements only the `tools.exec.deniedPaths` part.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`
- Scenarios the tests lock in:
  - direct `cat "<deniedFile>"` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'` is rejected;
  - sandbox `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key` from `/workspace` is rejected as `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec`;
  - node-host `type C:\Secrets\provider.key` is rejected after resolving the target node as `platform: "win32"` and before `system.run`;
  - node-host Windows denied-path matching is case-insensitive for `C:\Secrets\**` versus `c:\secrets\provider.key`;
  - `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` is runtime-resolved and blocks the selected HOME credential path;
  - config-loaded `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` stays authored through generic path normalization and blocks sandbox `HOME=/workspace`;
  - config-loaded `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` stays authored through generic path normalization and blocks Windows node `HOME=C:\Users\agent`;
  - `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` and shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` operands are expanded before matching and rejected.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises `createExecTool` directly and proves the preflight guard runs even when approvals are bypassed.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config key: `tools.exec.deniedPaths`. Commands that reference matching paths now fail before exec starts.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec command -> env/path setup -> host execution may receive /run/secrets/provider.key

After:
exec command -> env/path setup -> denied path preflight -> Security Violation before host execution
```

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` -> 119 passed
- `pnpm config:schema:check` -> ok
- `pnpm config:docs:check` -> ok
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> ok
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts src/config/io.ts` -> all matched files use correct format
- `git diff --check` -> ok

target.title: fix(deepseek): normalize mcp union tool schemas
target.body:
Fixes #83361.

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Bundle MCP tools could be materialized after provider tool-schema normalization, so DeepSeek requests could still receive raw MCP `anyOf`/`oneOf` schemas.
- DeepSeek rejects these schemas with `400 Invalid schema`, as reported for Unusual Whales MCP tools.
- This keeps the compatibility policy provider-owned by adding a DeepSeek tool-schema hook, then runs provider normalization over materialized bundled tools before they are included in the final effective tool set.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: DeepSeek-bound MCP tool schemas with `anyOf`/`oneOf` unions are normalized before provider submission, so the final tool parameter payload no longer contains the union keywords that triggered the reported DeepSeek `400 Invalid schema`.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the production provider-tool compatibility helper imported from `src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts`. This exercises the runtime schema payload that OpenClaw produces immediately before provider transport; no mocked provider response is involved in this command.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node --import tsx -e 'import { buildProviderToolCompatFamilyHooks } from "./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts"; const hooks = buildProviderToolCompatFamilyHooks("deepseek"); const tools = [{ name: "unusual-whales__get_balance_sheet_screener", description: "", parameters: { type: "object", properties: { date: { anyOf: [{ type: "string" }, { type: "integer" }] }, period: { oneOf: [{ type: "string" }, { type: "null" }] } }, required: ["date"] } }]; const normalized = hooks.normalizeToolSchemas({ provider: "deepseek", modelId: "deepseek-v4-pro", modelApi: "openai-completions", model: { provider: "deepseek", api: "openai-completions", id: "deepseek-v4-pro" }, tools }); console.log(JSON.stringify(normalized[0].parameters, null, 2)); console.log(JSON.stringify(hooks.inspectToolSchemas({ provider: "deepseek", modelId: "deepseek-v4-pro", modelApi: "openai-completions", model: { provider: "deepseek", api: "openai-completions", id: "deepseek-v4-pro" }, tools: normalized }), null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "date": {
      "type": "string"
    },
    "period": {
      "type": "string",
      "nullable": true
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "date"
  ]
}
[]
```

Observed result after fix: The Unusual Whales-style `date.anyOf` payload is emitted as `{ "type": "string" }`, the nullable `period.oneOf` payload is emitted as `{ "type": "string", "nullable": true }`, and DeepSeek schema diagnostics return `[]`. That is the provider-bound schema shape after the fix.

What was not tested: A live DeepSeek API request with a real Unusual Whales MCP server, because credentials and server access were not available in this environment.

## Validation
Focused provider/plugin validation:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  19 passed (19)
```

Full validation:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts

Test Files  9 passed (9)
Tests  96 passed (96)
```

Other checks:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/deepseek/index.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts
git diff --check
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```

Commit author verification:
```console
455941e999 Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> fix(deepseek): normalize mcp union tool schemas
```

target.title: Doctor: convert interactive maintenance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Converts the next interactive doctor maintenance bucket to the structured `detect()` / `repair()` health contract introduced by #83198 while preserving the original doctor contribution order:

- shell completion detection and cached-completion repair
- startup channel maintenance as a repair-only structured health check
- Linux systemd linger detection and guided repair for local Gateway services

The important follow-up in this revision is that converted repairs no longer run from a separate early `doctor:structured-health-repairs` contribution. Each converted contribution now invokes its mapped health check from its existing slot in the doctor order, so the contract changes without changing sequencing. Existing conversion metadata still advertises `healthCheckIds` for lint filtering and conversion tracking.

## Behavior

- `doctor --lint` can report shell-completion and systemd-linger findings without mutating state.
- `doctor --lint --only core/doctor/shell-completion` stays quiet when shell completion is simply not installed, because completion install is optional.
- `doctor --fix` runs converted `repair()` paths at the same point where the legacy contribution previously ran, then validates with the same scoped `detect()` logic.
- Startup channel maintenance remains repair-only: it is intentionally quiet in lint mode, but participates in the structured repair pipeline during `doctor --fix` at the startup-channel contribution position.
- Browser clawd-profile residue repair from the earlier conversion path is also kept at the browser contribution position rather than an early bulk pass.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Follow-up doctor maintenance checks migrate from legacy `run(ctx)` repair logic into structured health checks without changing the user-facing repair order; optional shell completion installs do not become lint failures.
- **Real environment tested**: WSL Ubuntu checkout cloned from `giodl73-repo/openclaw:doctor-detection-interactive-maintenance` after the latest push, plus the same patch copied into the WSL proof checkout for targeted verification before push.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
pnpm openclaw doctor --lint --only core/doctor/shell-completion --json

node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run \
  src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts \
  src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts \
  --reporter=dot

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts \
  src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.ts

git diff --check
```

Earlier focused conversion proof on this branch also ran:

```bash
CI=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 \
  pnpm test \
    src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts \
    src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts \
    src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts \
    src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts \
```
